title: Automated experimental design for high-probability entanglement generation
authors: Carlos Ruiz-Gonzalez, Mario Krenn, Xuemei Gu
abstract: Entangled photons are widely used in quantum technologies. Many photonic experiments generate them with probabilistic photon-pair sources that can be modeled as squeeze operators. In practice, these sources are usually treated in the low-gain (perturbative) regime, keeping only the leading single-pair term and neglecting higher-order multi-pair emission events. In pursuit of fidelity, the probability of successful entanglement generation can become extremely small, a tradeoff often ignored. Here we develop an automated design algorithm for quantum experiments to optimize both fidelity and success probability while accounting for higher-order multi-pair emissions. Our discovery algorithm explores different design topologies subject to varying hardware constraints. It optimizes the source parameters to reduce undesired higher-order terms or even benefit from them. The experiments presented outperform previous proposals for widely used states, including heralded Bell states, W states, and NOON states, paving the way for more efficient photonic technologies.
summary: This paper introduces an automated algorithm to design photonic experiments that maximize both the fidelity and the success probability of entanglement generation. By moving beyond the low-gain approximation and explicitly engineering multi-photon emissions, the method produces highly efficient setups for generating W, Bell, and NOON states.
main_problem: The fundamental tradeoff between fidelity and success probability in photonic entanglement generation, where increasing pump power to improve success probability leads to detrimental multi-pair emissions.
main_result: The development of an automated design algorithm that optimizes both fidelity and success probability by accounting for higher-order emissions, outperforming previous designs for W, Bell, and NOON states.
method: A discovery algorithm performing joint optimization over a discrete search space (source ordering) and a continuous parameter space (squeezing parameters) using automatic differentiation and Fock-basis modeling.
model_or_system: Photonic quantum technologies using Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC) sources, modeled as full squeeze operators in the Fock basis, combined with linear optical elements and various detection schemes.
key_observables: Fidelity (F), Success Probability (P), and expected counts per second.
important_parameters: Squeezing parameters (amplitude and phase), pump repetition rate, and photon-number resolving (PNR) vs. threshold detection regimes.
main_assumptions: The Hilbert space is truncated for computational feasibility; the algorithm uses randomized ordering which does not guarantee global optimality; the pump repetition rate is assumed to be 1-10ns.
figures_summary: Fig 1: GHZ state generation and the fidelity-probability tradeoff; Fig 2: The four-step optimization strategy; Fig 3: Performance comparison of new designs against benchmarks; Fig 4: Contribution of high-order photon terms to fidelity; Fig 5: Impact of source ordering on W states; Fig 6-7: Performance of different operator sequences for N00N states.
paper_structure: Introduction of the fidelity-probability tradeoff, description of the automated design algorithm and optimization strategy, demonstration of performance improvements for specific states (W, Bell, NOON), and an appendix validating the design against baseline benchmarks.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful framework for engineering non-classical states by utilizing higher-order photon statistics as a resource rather than a noise source, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and the development of scalable photonic quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Generating a 4-qubit GHZ state. (a) We repli- cate an entanglement generation experiment with SPDC crys- tals and threshold detectors [25]. When all detectors click, the projected state is a superposition of horizontally and ver- tically polarized photons from the blue (I & II) and orange (III & IV) sources, respectively. With two Fock modes, we encode the photons as |H⟩= |1, 0⟩and |V⟩= |0, 1⟩. (b) The SPDC crystals act as squeeze operators on 4 different optical paths, initialized at vacuum with 2 polarization modes: |0, 0⟩. Splitting each path into 2 modes, we see the sources commute, as they act on different modes. (c) Increasing the common pump r, we transcend the low-gain...

FIG. 2. Optimization strategy. The search takes four steps: 1) experiment optimization for random sortings and target fidelities f0 (only a few shown), 2) simplification of the best candidates computed with a higher photon cutoff (con- vergence check), 3) optimization of previous solutions slowly varying f0, and 4) final choice of best-performing designs.

FIG. 3. High probability entanglement by path identity. The presented experiments use SPDC crystals (squeeze operators) to generate entanglement. These path identity experiments are compared with previous designs, originally proposed for the low-gain regime, that neglected higher-order terms [16, 23, 24]. a) The first design produces a postselected W state of 4 qubits using threshold detectors. b) The second produces a heralded Bell state using 4 ancillary paths, each with a threshold detector. The entangled photons are not detected. c) The last one can generate two variants of N00N states using different parameters and postselection rules. For N=3, the threshold detector is not needed,...

FIG. 4. Relative contribution to the fidelity of high- order terms. When using ancilla paths, creation events with a higher photon-number can also lead to the target (reduced) state. Yet, lower-order terms are still predominant.

FIG. 5. 54 canonical sortings for the baseline design. When generating the W state, the sources’ ordering in the baseline design leads to different tradeoffs between fidelity and success probability. The positions of sources F and G are a good proxy for performance.
